nimbledeps/pkgs2/nim-2.2.10-17ec440fdb89f8903db29a17898af590087d2b64/lib/pure/strutils.nim

1 #
2 #
3 # Nim's Runtime Library
4 # (c) Copyright 2012 Andreas Rumpf
5 #
6 # See the file "copying.txt", included in this
7 # distribution, for details about the copyright.
8 #
9
10 ## The system module defines several common functions for working with strings,
11 ## such as:
12 ## * `$` for converting other data-types to strings
13 ## * `&` for string concatenation
14 ## * `add` for adding a new character or a string to the existing one
15 ## * `in` (alias for `contains`) and `notin` for checking if a character
16 ## is in a string
17 ##
18 ## This module builds upon that, providing additional functionality in form of
19 ## procedures, iterators and templates for strings.
20
21 runnableExamples:
22 let
23 numbers = @[867, 5309]
24 multiLineString = "first line\nsecond line\nthird line"
25
26 let jenny = numbers.join("-")
27 assert jenny == "867-5309"
28
29 assert splitLines(multiLineString) ==
30 @["first line", "second line", "third line"]
31 assert split(multiLineString) == @["first", "line", "second",
32 "line", "third", "line"]
33 assert indent(multiLineString, 4) ==
34 " first line\n second line\n third line"
35 assert 'z'.repeat(5) == "zzzzz"
36
37 ## The chaining of functions is possible thanks to the
38 ## `method call syntax<manual.html#procedures-method-call-syntax>`_:
39
40 runnableExamples:
41 from std/sequtils import map
42
43 let jenny = "867-5309"
44 assert jenny.split('-').map(parseInt) == @[867, 5309]
45
46 assert "Beetlejuice".indent(1).repeat(3).strip ==
47 "Beetlejuice Beetlejuice Beetlejuice"
48
49 ## This module is available for the `JavaScript target
50 ## <backends.html#backends-the-javascript-target>`_.
51 ##
52 ## ----
53 ##
54 ## **See also:**
55 ## * `strformat module<strformat.html>`_ for string interpolation and formatting
56 ## * `unicode module<unicode.html>`_ for Unicode UTF-8 handling
57 ## * `sequtils module<sequtils.html>`_ for operations on container
58 ## types (including strings)
59 ## * `parsecsv module<parsecsv.html>`_ for a high-performance CSV parser
60 ## * `parseutils module<parseutils.html>`_ for lower-level parsing of tokens,
61 ## numbers, identifiers, etc.
62 ## * `parseopt module<parseopt.html>`_ for command-line parsing
63 ## * `pegs module<pegs.html>`_ for PEG (Parsing Expression Grammar) support
64 ## * `strtabs module<strtabs.html>`_ for efficient hash tables
65 ## (dictionaries, in some programming languages) mapping from strings to strings
66 ## * `ropes module<ropes.html>`_ for rope data type, which can represent very
67 ## long strings efficiently
68 ## * `re module<re.html>`_ for regular expression (regex) support
69 ## * `strscans<strscans.html>`_ for `scanf` and `scanp` macros, which offer
70 ## easier substring extraction than regular expressions
71
72
73 import std/parseutils
74 from std/math import pow, floor, log10
75 from std/algorithm import fill, reverse
76 import std/enumutils
77 from std/bitops import fastLog2
78
79 from std/unicode import toLower, toUpper
80 export toLower, toUpper
81
82 include "system/inclrtl"
83 import std/private/[since, jsutils]
84 from std/private/strimpl import cmpIgnoreStyleImpl, cmpIgnoreCaseImpl,
85 startsWithImpl, endsWithImpl
86
87 when defined(nimPreviewSlimSystem):
88 import std/assertions
89
90
91 const
92 Whitespace* = {' ', '\t', '\v', '\r', '\l', '\f'}
93 ## All the characters that count as whitespace (space, tab, vertical tab,
94 ## carriage return, new line, form feed).
95
96 Letters* = {'A'..'Z', 'a'..'z'}
97 ## The set of letters.
98
99 UppercaseLetters* = {'A'..'Z'}
100 ## The set of uppercase ASCII letters.
101
102 LowercaseLetters* = {'a'..'z'}
103 ## The set of lowercase ASCII letters.
104
105 PunctuationChars* = {'!'..'/', ':'..'@', '['..'`', '{'..'~'}
106 ## The set of all ASCII punctuation characters.
107
108 Digits* = {'0'..'9'}
109 ## The set of digits.
110
111 HexDigits* = {'0'..'9', 'A'..'F', 'a'..'f'}
112 ## The set of hexadecimal digits.
113
114 IdentChars* = {'a'..'z', 'A'..'Z', '0'..'9', '_'}
115 ## The set of characters an identifier can consist of.
116
117 IdentStartChars* = {'a'..'z', 'A'..'Z', '_'}
118 ## The set of characters an identifier can start with.
119
120 Newlines* = {'\13', '\10'}
121 ## The set of characters a newline terminator can start with (carriage
122 ## return, line feed).
123
124 PrintableChars* = Letters + Digits + PunctuationChars + Whitespace
125 ## The set of all printable ASCII characters (letters, digits, whitespace, and punctuation characters).
126
127 AllChars* = {'\x00'..'\xFF'}
128 ## A set with all the possible characters.
129 ##
130 ## Not very useful by its own, you can use it to create *inverted* sets to
131 ## make the `find func<#find,string,set[char],Natural,int>`_
132 ## find **invalid** characters in strings. Example:
133 ## ```nim
134 ## let invalid = AllChars - Digits
135 ## doAssert "01234".find(invalid) == -1
136 ## doAssert "01A34".find(invalid) == 2
137 ## ```
138
139 func isAlphaAscii*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsAlphaAsciiChar".} =
140 ## Checks whether or not character `c` is alphabetical.
141 ##
142 ## This checks a-z, A-Z ASCII characters only.
143 ## Use `Unicode module<unicode.html>`_ for UTF-8 support.
144 runnableExamples:
145 doAssert isAlphaAscii('e') == true
146 doAssert isAlphaAscii('E') == true
147 doAssert isAlphaAscii('8') == false
148 return c in Letters
149
150 func isAlphaNumeric*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsAlphaNumericChar".} =
151 ## Checks whether or not `c` is alphanumeric.
152 ##
153 ## This checks a-z, A-Z, 0-9 ASCII characters only.
154 runnableExamples:
155 doAssert isAlphaNumeric('n') == true
156 doAssert isAlphaNumeric('8') == true
157 doAssert isAlphaNumeric(' ') == false
158 return c in Letters+Digits
159
160 func isDigit*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsDigitChar".} =
161 ## Checks whether or not `c` is a number.
162 ##
163 ## This checks 0-9 ASCII characters only.
164 runnableExamples:
165 doAssert isDigit('n') == false
166 doAssert isDigit('8') == true
167 return c in Digits
168
169 func isSpaceAscii*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsSpaceAsciiChar".} =
170 ## Checks whether or not `c` is a whitespace character.
171 runnableExamples:
172 doAssert isSpaceAscii('n') == false
173 doAssert isSpaceAscii(' ') == true
174 doAssert isSpaceAscii('\t') == true
175 return c in Whitespace
176
177 func isLowerAscii*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsLowerAsciiChar".} =
178 ## Checks whether or not `c` is a lower case character.
179 ##
180 ## This checks ASCII characters only.
181 ## Use `Unicode module<unicode.html>`_ for UTF-8 support.
182 ##
183 ## See also:
184 ## * `toLowerAscii func<#toLowerAscii,char>`_
185 runnableExamples:
186 doAssert isLowerAscii('e') == true
187 doAssert isLowerAscii('E') == false
188 doAssert isLowerAscii('7') == false
189 return c in LowercaseLetters
190
191 func isUpperAscii*(c: char): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuIsUpperAsciiChar".} =
192 ## Checks whether or not `c` is an upper case character.
193 ##
194 ## This checks ASCII characters only.
195 ## Use `Unicode module<unicode.html>`_ for UTF-8 support.
196 ##
197 ## See also:
198 ## * `toUpperAscii func<#toUpperAscii,char>`_
199 runnableExamples:
200 doAssert isUpperAscii('e') == false
201 doAssert isUpperAscii('E') == true
202 doAssert isUpperAscii('7') == false
203 return c in UppercaseLetters
204
205 func toLowerAscii*(c: char): char {.rtl, extern: "nsuToLowerAsciiChar".} =
206 ## Returns the lower case version of character `c`.
207 ##
208 ## This works only for the letters `A-Z`. See `unicode.toLower
209 ## <unicode.html#toLower,Rune>`_ for a version that works for any Unicode
210 ## character.
211 ##
212 ## See also:
213 ## * `isLowerAscii func<#isLowerAscii,char>`_
214 ## * `toLowerAscii func<#toLowerAscii,string>`_ for converting a string
215 runnableExamples:
216 doAssert toLowerAscii('A') == 'a'
217 doAssert toLowerAscii('e') == 'e'
218 if c in UppercaseLetters:
219 result = char(uint8(c) xor 0b0010_0000'u8)
220 else:
221 result = c
222
223 template toImpl(call) =
224 result = newString(len(s))
225 for i in 0..len(s) - 1:
226 result[i] = call(s[i])
227
228 func toLowerAscii*(s: string): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuToLowerAsciiStr".} =
229 ## Converts string `s` into lower case.
230 ##
231 ## This works only for the letters `A-Z`. See `unicode.toLower
232 ## <unicode.html#toLower,string>`_ for a version that works for any Unicode
233 ## character.
234 ##
235 ## See also:
236 ## * `normalize func<#normalize,string>`_
237 runnableExamples:
238 doAssert toLowerAscii("FooBar!") == "foobar!"
239 toImpl toLowerAscii
240
241 func toUpperAscii*(c: char): char {.rtl, extern: "nsuToUpperAsciiChar".} =
242 ## Converts character `c` into upper case.
243 ##
244 ## This works only for the letters `A-Z`. See `unicode.toUpper
245 ## <unicode.html#toUpper,Rune>`_ for a version that works for any Unicode
246 ## character.
247 ##
248 ## See also:
249 ## * `isUpperAscii func<#isUpperAscii,char>`_
250 ## * `toUpperAscii func<#toUpperAscii,string>`_ for converting a string
251 ## * `capitalizeAscii func<#capitalizeAscii,string>`_
252 runnableExamples:
253 doAssert toUpperAscii('a') == 'A'
254 doAssert toUpperAscii('E') == 'E'
255 if c in LowercaseLetters:
256 result = char(uint8(c) xor 0b0010_0000'u8)
257 else:
258 result = c
259
260 func toUpperAscii*(s: string): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuToUpperAsciiStr".} =
261 ## Converts string `s` into upper case.
262 ##
263 ## This works only for the letters `A-Z`. See `unicode.toUpper
264 ## <unicode.html#toUpper,string>`_ for a version that works for any Unicode
265 ## character.
266 ##
267 ## See also:
268 ## * `capitalizeAscii func<#capitalizeAscii,string>`_
269 runnableExamples:
270 doAssert toUpperAscii("FooBar!") == "FOOBAR!"
271 toImpl toUpperAscii
272
273 func capitalizeAscii*(s: string): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuCapitalizeAscii".} =
274 ## Converts the first character of string `s` into upper case.
275 ##
276 ## This works only for the letters `A-Z`.
277 ## Use `Unicode module<unicode.html>`_ for UTF-8 support.
278 ##
279 ## See also:
280 ## * `toUpperAscii func<#toUpperAscii,char>`_
281 runnableExamples:
282 doAssert capitalizeAscii("foo") == "Foo"
283 doAssert capitalizeAscii("-bar") == "-bar"
284 if s.len == 0: result = ""
285 else: result = toUpperAscii(s[0]) & substr(s, 1)
286
287 ✗ func nimIdentNormalize*(s: string): string =
288 ## Normalizes the string `s` as a Nim identifier.
289 ##
290 ## That means to convert to lower case and remove any '_' on all characters
291 ## except first one.
292 ##
293 ## .. Warning:: Backticks (`) are not handled: they remain *as is* and
294 ## spaces are preserved. See `nimIdentBackticksNormalize
295 ## <dochelpers.html#nimIdentBackticksNormalize,string>`_ for
296 ## an alternative approach.
297 runnableExamples:
298 doAssert nimIdentNormalize("Foo_bar") == "Foobar"
299 ✗ result = newString(s.len)
300 ✗ if s.len == 0:
301 ✗ return
302 ✗ result[0] = s[0]
303 ✗ var j = 1
304 ✗ for i in 1..len(s) - 1:
305 ✗ if s[i] in UppercaseLetters:
306 ✗ result[j] = chr(ord(s[i]) + (ord('a') - ord('A')))
307 ✗ inc j
308 ✗ elif s[i] != '_':
309 ✗ result[j] = s[i]
310 ✗ inc j
311 ✗ if j != s.len: setLen(result, j)
312 ✗
313 func normalize*(s: string): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuNormalize".} =
314 ✗ ## Normalizes the string `s`.
315 ✗ ##
316 ## That means to convert it to lower case and remove any '_'. This
317 ## should NOT be used to normalize Nim identifier names.
318 ##
319 ## See also:
320 ## * `toLowerAscii func<#toLowerAscii,string>`_
321 runnableExamples:
322 doAssert normalize("Foo_bar") == "foobar"
323 doAssert normalize("Foo Bar") == "foo bar"
324 result = newString(s.len)
325 var j = 0
326 for i in 0..len(s) - 1:
327 if s[i] in UppercaseLetters:
328 result[j] = chr(ord(s[i]) + (ord('a') - ord('A')))
329 inc j
330 elif s[i] != '_':
331 result[j] = s[i]
332 inc j
333 if j != s.len: setLen(result, j)
334
335 func cmpIgnoreCase*(a, b: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuCmpIgnoreCase".} =
336 ## Compares two strings in a case insensitive manner. Returns:
337 ##
338 ## | `0` if a == b
339 ## | `< 0` if a < b
340 ## | `> 0` if a > b
341 runnableExamples:
342 doAssert cmpIgnoreCase("FooBar", "foobar") == 0
343 doAssert cmpIgnoreCase("bar", "Foo") < 0
344 doAssert cmpIgnoreCase("Foo5", "foo4") > 0
345 cmpIgnoreCaseImpl(a, b)
346
347 {.push checks: off, line_trace: off.} # this is a hot-spot in the compiler!
348 # thus we compile without checks here
349
350 ✗ func cmpIgnoreStyle*(a, b: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuCmpIgnoreStyle".} =
351 ## Semantically the same as `cmp(normalize(a), normalize(b))`. It
352 ## is just optimized to not allocate temporary strings. This should
353 ## NOT be used to compare Nim identifier names.
354 ## Use `macros.eqIdent<macros.html#eqIdent,string,string>`_ for that.
355 ##
356 ## Returns:
357 ##
358 ## | `0` if a == b
359 ## | `< 0` if a < b
360 ## | `> 0` if a > b
361 runnableExamples:
362 doAssert cmpIgnoreStyle("foo_bar", "FooBar") == 0
363 doAssert cmpIgnoreStyle("foo_bar_5", "FooBar4") > 0
364 ✗ cmpIgnoreStyleImpl(a, b)
365 {.pop.}
366
367 # --------- Private templates for different split separators -----------
368
369 ✗ func substrEq(s: string, pos: int, substr: string): bool =
370 # Always returns false for empty `substr`
371 ✗ var length = substr.len
372 ✗ if length > 0:
373 ✗ var i = 0
374 ✗ while i < length and pos+i < s.len and s[pos+i] == substr[i]:
375 ✗ inc i
376 ✗ i == length
377 ✗ else: false
378 ✗
379 ✗ template stringHasSep(s: string, index: int, seps: set[char]): bool =
380 ✗ s[index] in seps
381
382 template stringHasSep(s: string, index: int, sep: char): bool =
383 s[index] == sep
384
385 template stringHasSep(s: string, index: int, sep: string): bool =
386 s.substrEq(index, sep)
387
388 template splitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, sepLen) =
389 ## Common code for split procs
390 var last = 0
391 ✗ var splits = maxsplit
392 ✗
393 ✗ while last <= len(s):
394 var first = last
395 ✗ while last < len(s) and not stringHasSep(s, last, sep):
396 ✗ inc(last)
397 ✗ if splits == 0: last = len(s)
398 ✗ yield substr(s, first, last-1)
399 ✗ if splits == 0: break
400 ✗ dec(splits)
401 ✗ inc(last, sepLen)
402 ✗
403 ✗ template oldSplit(s, seps, maxsplit) =
404 var last = 0
405 var splits = maxsplit
406 assert(not ('\0' in seps))
407 while last < len(s):
408 while last < len(s) and s[last] in seps: inc(last)
409 var first = last
410 while last < len(s) and s[last] notin seps: inc(last)
411 if first <= last-1:
412 if splits == 0: last = len(s)
413 yield substr(s, first, last-1)
414 if splits == 0: break
415 dec(splits)
416
417 template accResult(iter: untyped) =
418 ✗ result = @[]
419 ✗ for x in iter: add(result, x)
420
421
422 iterator split*(s: string, sep: char, maxsplit: int = -1): string =
423 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings using a single separator.
424 ##
425 ## Substrings are separated by the character `sep`.
426 ## The code:
427 ## ```nim
428 ## for word in split(";;this;is;an;;example;;;", ';'):
429 ## writeLine(stdout, word)
430 ## ```
431 ## Results in:
432 ## ```
433 ## ""
434 ## ""
435 ## "this"
436 ## "is"
437 ## "an"
438 ## ""
439 ## "example"
440 ## ""
441 ## ""
442 ## ""
443 ## ```
444 ##
445 ## See also:
446 ## * `rsplit iterator<#rsplit.i,string,char,int>`_
447 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
448 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
449 ## * `split func<#split,string,char,int>`_
450 ✗ splitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, 1)
451
452 iterator split*(s: string, seps: set[char] = Whitespace,
453 maxsplit: int = -1): string =
454 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings using a group of separators.
455 ##
456 ## Substrings are separated by a substring containing only `seps`.
457 ##
458 ## ```nim
459 ## for word in split("this\lis an\texample"):
460 ## writeLine(stdout, word)
461 ## ```
462 ##
463 ## ...generates this output:
464 ##
465 ## ```
466 ## "this"
467 ## "is"
468 ## "an"
469 ## "example"
470 ## ```
471 ##
472 ## And the following code:
473 ##
474 ## ```nim
475 ## for word in split("this:is;an$example", {';', ':', '$'}):
476 ## writeLine(stdout, word)
477 ## ```
478 ##
479 ## ...produces the same output as the first example. The code:
480 ##
481 ## ```nim
482 ## let date = "2012-11-20T22:08:08.398990"
483 ## let separators = {' ', '-', ':', 'T'}
484 ## for number in split(date, separators):
485 ## writeLine(stdout, number)
486 ## ```
487 ##
488 ## ...results in:
489 ##
490 ## ```
491 ## "2012"
492 ## "11"
493 ## "20"
494 ## "22"
495 ## "08"
496 ## "08.398990"
497 ## ```
498 ##
499 ## .. note:: Empty separator set results in returning an original string,
500 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
501 ##
502 ## See also:
503 ## * `rsplit iterator<#rsplit.i,string,set[char],int>`_
504 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
505 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
506 ## * `split func<#split,string,set[char],int>`_
507 splitCommon(s, seps, maxsplit, 1)
508
509 iterator split*(s: string, sep: string, maxsplit: int = -1): string =
510 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings using a string separator.
511 ##
512 ## Substrings are separated by the string `sep`.
513 ## The code:
514 ##
515 ## ```nim
516 ## for word in split("thisDATAisDATAcorrupted", "DATA"):
517 ## writeLine(stdout, word)
518 ## ```
519 ##
520 ## Results in:
521 ##
522 ## ```
523 ## "this"
524 ## "is"
525 ## "corrupted"
526 ## ```
527 ##
528 ## .. note:: Empty separator string results in returning an original string,
529 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
530 ##
531 ## See also:
532 ## * `rsplit iterator<#rsplit.i,string,string,int,bool>`_
533 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
534 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
535 ## * `split func<#split,string,string,int>`_
536 let sepLen = if sep.len == 0: 1 # prevents infinite loop
537 ✗ else: sep.len
538 ✗ splitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, sepLen)
539 ✗
540
541 template rsplitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, sepLen) =
542 ## Common code for rsplit functions
543 var
544 last = s.len - 1
545 first = last
546 splits = maxsplit
547 startPos = 0
548 # go to -1 in order to get separators at the beginning
549 while first >= -1:
550 while first >= 0 and not stringHasSep(s, first, sep):
551 dec(first)
552 if splits == 0:
553 # No more splits means set first to the beginning
554 first = -1
555 if first == -1:
556 startPos = 0
557 else:
558 startPos = first + sepLen
559 yield substr(s, startPos, last)
560 if splits == 0: break
561 dec(splits)
562 dec(first)
563 last = first
564
565 iterator rsplit*(s: string, sep: char,
566 maxsplit: int = -1): string =
567 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings from the right using a
568 ## string separator. Works exactly the same as `split iterator
569 ## <#split.i,string,char,int>`_ except in **reverse** order.
570 ##
571 ## ```nim
572 ## for piece in "foo:bar".rsplit(':'):
573 ## echo piece
574 ## ```
575 ##
576 ## Results in:
577 ##
578 ## ```
579 ## "bar"
580 ## "foo"
581 ## ```
582 ##
583 ## Substrings are separated from the right by the char `sep`.
584 ##
585 ## See also:
586 ## * `split iterator<#split.i,string,char,int>`_
587 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
588 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
589 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,char,int>`_
590 rsplitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, 1)
591
592 iterator rsplit*(s: string, seps: set[char] = Whitespace,
593 maxsplit: int = -1): string =
594 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings from the right using a
595 ## string separator. Works exactly the same as `split iterator
596 ## <#split.i,string,char,int>`_ except in **reverse** order.
597 ##
598 ## ```nim
599 ## for piece in "foo bar".rsplit(WhiteSpace):
600 ## echo piece
601 ## ```
602 ##
603 ## Results in:
604 ##
605 ## ```
606 ## "bar"
607 ## "foo"
608 ## ```
609 ##
610 ## Substrings are separated from the right by the set of chars `seps`
611 ##
612 ## .. note:: Empty separator set results in returning an original string,
613 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
614 ##
615 ## See also:
616 ## * `split iterator<#split.i,string,set[char],int>`_
617 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
618 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
619 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,set[char],int>`_
620 rsplitCommon(s, seps, maxsplit, 1)
621
622 iterator rsplit*(s: string, sep: string, maxsplit: int = -1,
623 keepSeparators: bool = false): string =
624 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings from the right using a
625 ## string separator. Works exactly the same as `split iterator
626 ## <#split.i,string,string,int>`_ except in **reverse** order.
627 ##
628 ## ```nim
629 ## for piece in "foothebar".rsplit("the"):
630 ## echo piece
631 ## ```
632 ##
633 ## Results in:
634 ##
635 ## ```
636 ## "bar"
637 ## "foo"
638 ## ```
639 ##
640 ## Substrings are separated from the right by the string `sep`
641 ##
642 ## .. note:: Empty separator string results in returning an original string,
643 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
644 ##
645 ## See also:
646 ## * `split iterator<#split.i,string,string,int>`_
647 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
648 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
649 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,string,int>`_
650 let sepLen = if sep.len == 0: 1 # prevents infinite loop
651 else: sep.len
652 rsplitCommon(s, sep, maxsplit, sepLen)
653
654 iterator splitLines*(s: string, keepEol = false): string =
655 ## Splits the string `s` into its containing lines.
656 ##
657 ## Every `character literal <manual.html#lexical-analysis-character-literals>`_
658 ## newline combination (CR, LF, CR-LF) is supported. The result strings
659 ## contain no trailing end of line characters unless the parameter `keepEol`
660 ## is set to `true`.
661 ##
662 ## Example:
663 ##
664 ## ```nim
665 ## for line in splitLines("\nthis\nis\nan\n\nexample\n"):
666 ## writeLine(stdout, line)
667 ## ```
668 ##
669 ## Results in:
670 ##
671 ## ```nim
672 ## ""
673 ## "this"
674 ## "is"
675 ## "an"
676 ## ""
677 ## "example"
678 ## ""
679 ## ```
680 ##
681 ## See also:
682 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator<#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
683 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
684 var first = 0
685 var last = 0
686 var eolpos = 0
687 while true:
688 while last < s.len and s[last] notin {'\c', '\l'}: inc(last)
689
690 eolpos = last
691 if last < s.len:
692 if s[last] == '\l': inc(last)
693 elif s[last] == '\c':
694 inc(last)
695 if last < s.len and s[last] == '\l': inc(last)
696
697 yield substr(s, first, if keepEol: last-1 else: eolpos-1)
698
699 # no eol characters consumed means that the string is over
700 if eolpos == last:
701 break
702
703 first = last
704
705 iterator splitWhitespace*(s: string, maxsplit: int = -1): string =
706 ## Splits the string `s` at whitespace stripping leading and trailing
707 ## whitespace if necessary. If `maxsplit` is specified and is positive,
708 ## no more than `maxsplit` splits is made.
709 ##
710 ## The following code:
711 ##
712 ## ```nim
713 ## let s = " foo \t bar baz "
714 ## for ms in [-1, 1, 2, 3]:
715 ## echo "------ maxsplit = ", ms, ":"
716 ## for item in s.splitWhitespace(maxsplit=ms):
717 ## echo '"', item, '"'
718 ## ```
719 ##
720 ## ...results in:
721 ##
722 ## ```
723 ## ------ maxsplit = -1:
724 ## "foo"
725 ## "bar"
726 ## "baz"
727 ## ------ maxsplit = 1:
728 ## "foo"
729 ## "bar baz "
730 ## ------ maxsplit = 2:
731 ## "foo"
732 ## "bar"
733 ## "baz "
734 ## ------ maxsplit = 3:
735 ## "foo"
736 ## "bar"
737 ## "baz"
738 ## ```
739 ##
740 ## See also:
741 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
742 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
743 oldSplit(s, Whitespace, maxsplit)
744
745
746
747 ✗ func split*(s: string, sep: char, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string] {.rtl,
748 extern: "nsuSplitChar".} =
749 ## The same as the `split iterator <#split.i,string,char,int>`_ (see its
750 ## documentation), but is a func that returns a sequence of substrings.
751 ##
752 ## See also:
753 ## * `split iterator <#split.i,string,char,int>`_
754 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,char,int>`_
755 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
756 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
757 runnableExamples:
758 doAssert "a,b,c".split(',') == @["a", "b", "c"]
759 doAssert "".split(' ') == @[""]
760 ✗ accResult(split(s, sep, maxsplit))
761
762 func split*(s: string, seps: set[char] = Whitespace, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[
763 string] {.rtl, extern: "nsuSplitCharSet".} =
764 ## The same as the `split iterator <#split.i,string,set[char],int>`_ (see its
765 ## documentation), but is a func that returns a sequence of substrings.
766 ##
767 ## .. note:: Empty separator set results in returning an original string,
768 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
769 ##
770 ## See also:
771 ## * `split iterator <#split.i,string,set[char],int>`_
772 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,set[char],int>`_
773 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
774 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
775 runnableExamples:
776 doAssert "a,b;c".split({',', ';'}) == @["a", "b", "c"]
777 doAssert "".split({' '}) == @[""]
778 doAssert "empty seps return unsplit s".split({}) == @["empty seps return unsplit s"]
779 accResult(split(s, seps, maxsplit))
780
781 ✗ func split*(s: string, sep: string, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string] {.rtl,
782 extern: "nsuSplitString".} =
783 ## Splits the string `s` into substrings using a string separator.
784 ##
785 ## Substrings are separated by the string `sep`. This is a wrapper around the
786 ## `split iterator <#split.i,string,string,int>`_.
787 ##
788 ## .. note:: Empty separator string results in returning an original string,
789 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
790 ##
791 ## See also:
792 ## * `split iterator <#split.i,string,string,int>`_
793 ## * `rsplit func<#rsplit,string,string,int>`_
794 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
795 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
796 runnableExamples:
797 doAssert "a,b,c".split(",") == @["a", "b", "c"]
798 doAssert "a man a plan a canal panama".split("a ") == @["", "man ", "plan ", "canal panama"]
799 doAssert "".split("Elon Musk") == @[""]
800 doAssert "a largely spaced sentence".split(" ") == @["a", "", "largely",
801 "", "", "", "spaced", "sentence"]
802 doAssert "a largely spaced sentence".split(" ", maxsplit = 1) == @["a", " largely spaced sentence"]
803 doAssert "empty sep returns unsplit s".split("") == @["empty sep returns unsplit s"]
804 ✗ accResult(split(s, sep, maxsplit))
805
806 func rsplit*(s: string, sep: char, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string] {.rtl,
807 extern: "nsuRSplitChar".} =
808 ## The same as the `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,char,int>`_, but is a func
809 ## that returns a sequence of substrings in original order.
810 ##
811 ## A possible common use case for `rsplit` is path manipulation,
812 ## particularly on systems that don't use a common delimiter.
813 ##
814 ## For example, if a system had `#` as a delimiter, you could
815 ## do the following to get the tail of the path:
816 ##
817 ## ```nim
818 ## var tailSplit = rsplit("Root#Object#Method#Index", '#', maxsplit=1)
819 ## ```
820 ##
821 ## Results in `tailSplit` containing:
822 ##
823 ## ```nim
824 ## @["Root#Object#Method", "Index"]
825 ## ```
826 ##
827 ## See also:
828 ## * `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,char,int>`_
829 ## * `split func<#split,string,char,int>`_
830 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
831 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
832 accResult(rsplit(s, sep, maxsplit))
833 result.reverse()
834
835 func rsplit*(s: string, seps: set[char] = Whitespace,
836 maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string]
837 {.rtl, extern: "nsuRSplitCharSet".} =
838 ## The same as the `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,set[char],int>`_, but is a
839 ## func that returns a sequence of substrings in original order.
840 ##
841 ## A possible common use case for `rsplit` is path manipulation,
842 ## particularly on systems that don't use a common delimiter.
843 ##
844 ## For example, if a system had `#` as a delimiter, you could
845 ## do the following to get the tail of the path:
846 ##
847 ## ```nim
848 ## var tailSplit = rsplit("Root#Object#Method#Index", {'#'}, maxsplit=1)
849 ## ```
850 ##
851 ## Results in `tailSplit` containing:
852 ##
853 ## ```nim
854 ## @["Root#Object#Method", "Index"]
855 ## ```
856 ##
857 ## .. note:: Empty separator set results in returning an original string,
858 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
859 ##
860 ## See also:
861 ## * `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,set[char],int>`_
862 ## * `split func<#split,string,set[char],int>`_
863 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
864 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
865 accResult(rsplit(s, seps, maxsplit))
866 result.reverse()
867
868 func rsplit*(s: string, sep: string, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string] {.rtl,
869 extern: "nsuRSplitString".} =
870 ## The same as the `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,string,int,bool>`_, but is a func
871 ## that returns a sequence of substrings in original order.
872 ##
873 ## A possible common use case for `rsplit` is path manipulation,
874 ## particularly on systems that don't use a common delimiter.
875 ##
876 ## For example, if a system had `#` as a delimiter, you could
877 ## do the following to get the tail of the path:
878 ##
879 ## ```nim
880 ## var tailSplit = rsplit("Root#Object#Method#Index", "#", maxsplit=1)
881 ## ```
882 ##
883 ## Results in `tailSplit` containing:
884 ##
885 ## ```nim
886 ## @["Root#Object#Method", "Index"]
887 ## ```
888 ##
889 ## .. note:: Empty separator string results in returning an original string,
890 ## following the interpretation "split by no element".
891 ##
892 ## See also:
893 ## * `rsplit iterator <#rsplit.i,string,string,int,bool>`_
894 ## * `split func<#split,string,string,int>`_
895 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
896 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
897 runnableExamples:
898 doAssert "a largely spaced sentence".rsplit(" ", maxsplit = 1) == @[
899 "a largely spaced", "sentence"]
900 doAssert "a,b,c".rsplit(",") == @["a", "b", "c"]
901 doAssert "a man a plan a canal panama".rsplit("a ") == @["", "man ",
902 "plan ", "canal panama"]
903 doAssert "".rsplit("Elon Musk") == @[""]
904 doAssert "a largely spaced sentence".rsplit(" ") == @["a", "",
905 "largely", "", "", "", "spaced", "sentence"]
906 doAssert "empty sep returns unsplit s".rsplit("") == @["empty sep returns unsplit s"]
907 accResult(rsplit(s, sep, maxsplit))
908 result.reverse()
909
910 func splitLines*(s: string, keepEol = false): seq[string] {.rtl,
911 extern: "nsuSplitLines".} =
912 ## The same as the `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_ (see its
913 ## documentation), but is a func that returns a sequence of substrings.
914 ##
915 ## See also:
916 ## * `splitLines iterator<#splitLines.i,string>`_
917 ## * `splitWhitespace func<#splitWhitespace,string,int>`_
918 ## * `countLines func<#countLines,string>`_
919 accResult(splitLines(s, keepEol = keepEol))
920
921 func splitWhitespace*(s: string, maxsplit: int = -1): seq[string] {.rtl,
922 extern: "nsuSplitWhitespace".} =
923 ## The same as the `splitWhitespace iterator <#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
924 ## (see its documentation), but is a func that returns a sequence of substrings.
925 ##
926 ## See also:
927 ## * `splitWhitespace iterator <#splitWhitespace.i,string,int>`_
928 ## * `splitLines func<#splitLines,string>`_
929 accResult(splitWhitespace(s, maxsplit))
930
931 func toBin*(x: BiggestInt, len: Positive): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuToBin".} =
932 ## Converts `x` into its binary representation.
933 ##
934 ## The resulting string is always `len` characters long. No leading `0b`
935 ## prefix is generated.
936 runnableExamples:
937 let
938 a = 29
939 b = 257
940 doAssert a.toBin(8) == "00011101"
941 doAssert b.toBin(8) == "00000001"
942 doAssert b.toBin(9) == "100000001"
943 var
944 mask = BiggestUInt 1
945 shift = BiggestUInt 0
946 assert(len > 0)
947 result = newString(len)
948 for j in countdown(len-1, 0):
949 result[j] = chr(int((BiggestUInt(x) and mask) shr shift) + ord('0'))
950 inc shift
951 mask = mask shl BiggestUInt(1)
952
953 func toOct*(x: BiggestInt, len: Positive): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuToOct".} =
954 ## Converts `x` into its octal representation.
955 ##
956 ## The resulting string is always `len` characters long. No leading `0o`
957 ## prefix is generated.
958 ##
959 ## Do not confuse it with `toOctal func<#toOctal,char>`_.
960 runnableExamples:
961 let
962 a = 62
963 b = 513
964 doAssert a.toOct(3) == "076"
965 doAssert b.toOct(3) == "001"
966 doAssert b.toOct(5) == "01001"
967 var
968 mask = BiggestUInt 7
969 shift = BiggestUInt 0
970 assert(len > 0)
971 result = newString(len)
972 for j in countdown(len-1, 0):
973 result[j] = chr(int((BiggestUInt(x) and mask) shr shift) + ord('0'))
974 inc shift, 3
975 mask = mask shl BiggestUInt(3)
976
977 16 func toHexImpl(x: BiggestUInt, len: Positive, handleNegative: bool): string =
978 const
979 HexChars = "0123456789ABCDEF"
980 16 var n = x
981 16 result = newString(len)
982

✓ Branch 6 taken 128 times.
✗ Branch 7 not taken.
✗ Branch 8 not taken.
✓ Branch 9 taken 128 times.
 128 result[j] = HexChars[int(n and 0xF)]

985 # handle negative overflow
986

 128 if n == 0 and handleNegative: n = not(BiggestUInt 0)

✗ Branch 1 not taken.
strutils::toHex(uInt32, range19223372036854775807):
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 128 times.
 144 func toHex*[T: SomeInteger](x: T, len: Positive): string =
989 ✗ ## Converts `x` to its hexadecimal representation.
990 ##
991 ## The resulting string will be exactly `len` characters long. No prefix like
992 ## `0x` is generated. `x` is treated as an unsigned value.
993 runnableExamples:
994 let
995 a = 62'u64
996 b = 4097'u64
997 doAssert a.toHex(3) == "03E"
998 doAssert b.toHex(3) == "001"
999 doAssert b.toHex(4) == "1001"
1000 doAssert toHex(62, 3) == "03E"
1001 doAssert toHex(-8, 6) == "FFFFF8"
1002 when jsNoBigInt64:
1003 toHexImpl(cast[BiggestUInt](x), len, x < 0)
1004 else:
1005 when T is SomeSignedInt:
1006 ✗ toHexImpl(cast[BiggestUInt](BiggestInt(x)), len, x < 0)
1007 ✗ else:
1008
1/4
strutils::toHex(int, range19223372036854775807):
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✗ Branch 1 not taken.
strutils::toHex(uInt32, range19223372036854775807):
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 16 times.
 16 toHexImpl(BiggestUInt(x), len, x < 0)
1009 16
1010 func toHex*[T: SomeInteger](x: T): string =
1011 ## Shortcut for `toHex(x, T.sizeof * 2)`
1012 runnableExamples:
1013 doAssert toHex(1984'i64) == "00000000000007C0"
1014 doAssert toHex(1984'i16) == "07C0"
1015 when jsNoBigInt64:
1016 toHexImpl(cast[BiggestUInt](x), 2*sizeof(T), x < 0)
1017 else:
1018 when T is SomeSignedInt:
1019 toHexImpl(cast[BiggestUInt](BiggestInt(x)), 2*sizeof(T), x < 0)
1020 else:
1021 toHexImpl(BiggestUInt(x), 2*sizeof(T), x < 0)
1022
1023 func toHex*(s: string): string {.rtl.} =
1024 ## Converts a bytes string to its hexadecimal representation.
1025 ##
1026 ## The output is twice the input long. No prefix like
1027 ## `0x` is generated.
1028 ##
1029 ## See also:
1030 ## * `parseHexStr func<#parseHexStr,string>`_ for the reverse operation
1031 runnableExamples:
1032 let
1033 a = "1"
1034 b = "A"
1035 c = "\0\255"
1036 doAssert a.toHex() == "31"
1037 doAssert b.toHex() == "41"
1038 doAssert c.toHex() == "00FF"
1039
1040 const HexChars = "0123456789ABCDEF"
1041 result = newString(s.len * 2)
1042 for pos, c in s:
1043 var n = ord(c)
1044 result[pos * 2 + 1] = HexChars[n and 0xF]
1045 n = n shr 4
1046 result[pos * 2] = HexChars[n]
1047
1048 func toOctal*(c: char): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuToOctal".} =
1049 ## Converts a character `c` to its octal representation.
1050 ##
1051 ## The resulting string may not have a leading zero. Its length is always
1052 ## exactly 3.
1053 ##
1054 ## Do not confuse it with `toOct func<#toOct,BiggestInt,Positive>`_.
1055 runnableExamples:
1056 doAssert toOctal('1') == "061"
1057 doAssert toOctal('A') == "101"
1058 doAssert toOctal('a') == "141"
1059 doAssert toOctal('!') == "041"
1060
1061 result = newString(3)
1062 var val = ord(c)
1063 for i in countdown(2, 0):
1064 result[i] = chr(val mod 8 + ord('0'))
1065 val = val div 8
1066
1067 func fromBin*[T: SomeInteger](s: string): T =
1068 ## Parses a binary integer value from a string `s`.
1069 ##
1070 ## If `s` is not a valid binary integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have
1071 ## one of the following optional prefixes: `0b`, `0B`. Underscores within
1072 ## `s` are ignored.
1073 ##
1074 ## Does not check for overflow. If the value represented by `s`
1075 ## is too big to fit into a return type, only the value of the rightmost
1076 ## binary digits of `s` is returned without producing an error.
1077 runnableExamples:
1078 let s = "0b_0100_1000_1000_1000_1110_1110_1001_1001"
1079 doAssert fromBin[int](s) == 1216933529
1080 doAssert fromBin[int8](s) == 0b1001_1001'i8
1081 doAssert fromBin[int8](s) == -103'i8
1082 doAssert fromBin[uint8](s) == 153
1083 doAssert s.fromBin[:int16] == 0b1110_1110_1001_1001'i16
1084 doAssert s.fromBin[:uint64] == 1216933529'u64
1085 result = T(0)
1086 let p = parseutils.parseBin(s, result)
1087 if p != s.len or p == 0:
1088 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid binary integer: " & s)
1089
1090 func fromOct*[T: SomeInteger](s: string): T =
1091 ## Parses an octal integer value from a string `s`.
1092 ##
1093 ## If `s` is not a valid octal integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have
1094 ## one of the following optional prefixes: `0o`, `0O`. Underscores within
1095 ## `s` are ignored.
1096 ##
1097 ## Does not check for overflow. If the value represented by `s`
1098 ## is too big to fit into a return type, only the value of the rightmost
1099 ## octal digits of `s` is returned without producing an error.
1100 runnableExamples:
1101 let s = "0o_123_456_777"
1102 doAssert fromOct[int](s) == 21913087
1103 doAssert fromOct[int8](s) == 0o377'i8
1104 doAssert fromOct[int8](s) == -1'i8
1105 doAssert fromOct[uint8](s) == 255'u8
1106 doAssert s.fromOct[:int16] == 24063'i16
1107 doAssert s.fromOct[:uint64] == 21913087'u64
1108 result = T(0)
1109 let p = parseutils.parseOct(s, result)
1110 if p != s.len or p == 0:
1111 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid oct integer: " & s)
1112
1113 func fromHex*[T: SomeInteger](s: string): T =
1114 ## Parses a hex integer value from a string `s`.
1115 ##
1116 ## If `s` is not a valid hex integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have
1117 ## one of the following optional prefixes: `0x`, `0X`, `#`. Underscores within
1118 ## `s` are ignored.
1119 ##
1120 ## Does not check for overflow. If the value represented by `s`
1121 ## is too big to fit into a return type, only the value of the rightmost
1122 ## hex digits of `s` is returned without producing an error.
1123 runnableExamples:
1124 let s = "0x_1235_8df6"
1125 doAssert fromHex[int](s) == 305499638
1126 doAssert fromHex[int8](s) == 0xf6'i8
1127 doAssert fromHex[int8](s) == -10'i8
1128 doAssert fromHex[uint8](s) == 246'u8
1129 doAssert s.fromHex[:int16] == -29194'i16
1130 doAssert s.fromHex[:uint64] == 305499638'u64
1131 result = T(0)
1132 let p = parseutils.parseHex(s, result)
1133 if p != s.len or p == 0:
1134 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid hex integer: " & s)
1135
1136 7 func intToStr*(x: int, minchars: Positive = 1): string {.rtl,
1137 extern: "nsuIntToStr".} =
1138 ## Converts `x` to its decimal representation.
1139 ##
1140 ## The resulting string will be minimally `minchars` characters long. This is
1141 ## achieved by adding leading zeros.
1142 runnableExamples:
1143 doAssert intToStr(1984) == "1984"
1144 doAssert intToStr(1984, 6) == "001984"
1145
4/6
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 7 times.
✓ Branch 2 taken 5 times.
✓ Branch 3 taken 2 times.
✗ Branch 4 not taken.
✓ Branch 5 taken 7 times.
 7 result = $abs(x)
1146
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 7 times.
 12 for i in 1 .. minchars - len(result):
1147 5 result = '0' & result
1148
1/2
✓ Branch 0 taken 7 times.
✗ Branch 1 not taken.
 7 if x < 0:
1149 ✗ result = '-' & result
1150
1151 func parseInt*(s: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseInt".} =
1152 ## Parses a decimal integer value contained in `s`.
1153 ##
1154 ## If `s` is not a valid integer, `ValueError` is raised.
1155 runnableExamples:
1156 doAssert parseInt("-0042") == -42
1157 result = 0
1158 let L = parseutils.parseInt(s, result, 0)
1159 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1160 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid integer: " & s)
1161
1162 ✗ func parseBiggestInt*(s: string): BiggestInt {.rtl,
1163 extern: "nsuParseBiggestInt".} =
1164 ## Parses a decimal integer value contained in `s`.
1165 ##
1166 ## If `s` is not a valid integer, `ValueError` is raised.
1167 ✗ result = BiggestInt(0)
1168 ✗ let L = parseutils.parseBiggestInt(s, result, 0)
1169 ✗ if L != s.len or L == 0:
1170 ✗ raise newException(ValueError, "invalid integer: " & s)
1171 ✗
1172 ✗ func parseUInt*(s: string): uint {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseUInt".} =
1173 ✗ ## Parses a decimal unsigned integer value contained in `s`.
1174 ##
1175 ## If `s` is not a valid integer, `ValueError` is raised.
1176 result = uint(0)
1177 let L = parseutils.parseUInt(s, result, 0)
1178 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1179 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid unsigned integer: " & s)
1180
1181 func parseBiggestUInt*(s: string): BiggestUInt {.rtl,
1182 extern: "nsuParseBiggestUInt".} =
1183 ## Parses a decimal unsigned integer value contained in `s`.
1184 ##
1185 ## If `s` is not a valid integer, `ValueError` is raised.
1186 result = BiggestUInt(0)
1187 let L = parseutils.parseBiggestUInt(s, result, 0)
1188 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1189 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid unsigned integer: " & s)
1190
1191 ✗ func parseFloat*(s: string): float {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseFloat".} =
1192 ## Parses a decimal floating point value contained in `s`.
1193 ##
1194 ## If `s` is not a valid floating point number, `ValueError` is raised.
1195 ##`NAN`, `INF`, `-INF` are also supported (case insensitive comparison).
1196 runnableExamples:
1197 doAssert parseFloat("3.14") == 3.14
1198 doAssert parseFloat("inf") == 1.0/0
1199 ✗ result = 0.0
1200 ✗ let L = parseutils.parseFloat(s, result, 0)
1201 ✗ if L != s.len or L == 0:
1202 ✗ raise newException(ValueError, "invalid float: " & s)
1203 ✗
1204 ✗ func parseBinInt*(s: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseBinInt".} =
1205 ✗ ## Parses a binary integer value contained in `s`.
1206 ##
1207 ## If `s` is not a valid binary integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have
1208 ## one of the following optional prefixes: `0b`, `0B`. Underscores within
1209 ## `s` are ignored.
1210 runnableExamples:
1211 let
1212 a = "0b11_0101"
1213 b = "111"
1214 doAssert a.parseBinInt() == 53
1215 doAssert b.parseBinInt() == 7
1216
1217 result = 0
1218 let L = parseutils.parseBin(s, result, 0)
1219 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1220 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid binary integer: " & s)
1221
1222 func parseOctInt*(s: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseOctInt".} =
1223 ## Parses an octal integer value contained in `s`.
1224 ##
1225 ## If `s` is not a valid oct integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have one
1226 ## of the following optional prefixes: `0o`, `0O`. Underscores within
1227 ## `s` are ignored.
1228 result = 0
1229 let L = parseutils.parseOct(s, result, 0)
1230 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1231 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid oct integer: " & s)
1232
1233 func parseHexInt*(s: string): int {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseHexInt".} =
1234 ## Parses a hexadecimal integer value contained in `s`.
1235 ##
1236 ## If `s` is not a valid hex integer, `ValueError` is raised. `s` can have one
1237 ## of the following optional prefixes: `0x`, `0X`, `#`. Underscores
1238 ## within `s` are ignored.
1239 result = 0
1240 let L = parseutils.parseHex(s, result, 0)
1241 if L != s.len or L == 0:
1242 raise newException(ValueError, "invalid hex integer: " & s)
1243
1244 func generateHexCharToValueMap(): string =
1245 ## Generates a string to map a hex digit to uint value.
1246 result = ""
1247 for inp in 0..255:
1248 let ch = chr(inp)
1249 let o =
1250 case ch
1251 of '0'..'9': inp - ord('0')
1252 of 'a'..'f': inp - ord('a') + 10
1253 of 'A'..'F': inp - ord('A') + 10
1254 else: 17 # indicates an invalid hex char
1255 result.add chr(o)
1256
1257 const hexCharToValueMap = generateHexCharToValueMap()
1258
1259 func parseHexStr*(s: string): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuParseHexStr".} =
1260 ## Converts hex-encoded string to byte string, e.g.:
1261 ##
1262 ## Raises `ValueError` for an invalid hex values. The comparison is
1263 ## case-insensitive.
1264 ##
1265 ## See also:
1266 ## * `toHex func<#toHex,string>`_ for the reverse operation
1267 runnableExamples:
1268 let
1269 a = "41"
1270 b = "3161"
1271 c = "00ff"
1272 doAssert parseHexStr(a) == "A"
1273 doAssert parseHexStr(b) == "1a"
1274 doAssert parseHexStr(c) == "\0\255"
1275
1276 if s.len mod 2 != 0:
1277 raise newException(ValueError, "Incorrect hex string len")
1278 result = newString(s.len div 2)
1279 var buf = 0
1280 for pos, c in s:
1281 let val = hexCharToValueMap[ord(c)].ord
1282 if val == 17:
1283 raise newException(ValueError, "Invalid hex char `" &
1284 c & "` (ord " & $c.ord & ")")
1285 if pos mod 2 == 0:
1286 buf = val
1287 else:
1288 result[pos div 2] = chr(val + buf shl 4)
1289
1290 func parseBool*(s: string): bool =
1291 ## Parses a value into a `bool`.
1292 ##
1293 ## If `s` is one of the following values: `y, yes, true, 1, on`, then
1294 ## returns `true`. If `s` is one of the following values: `n, no, false,
1295 ## 0, off`, then returns `false`. If `s` is something else a
1296 ## `ValueError` exception is raised.
1297 runnableExamples:
1298 let a = "n"
1299 doAssert parseBool(a) == false
1300
1301 case normalize(s)
1302 of "y", "yes", "true", "1", "on": result = true
1303 of "n", "no", "false", "0", "off": result = false
1304 else: raise newException(ValueError, "cannot interpret as a bool: " & s)
1305
1306 ✗ func parseEnum*[T: enum](s: string): T =
1307 ## Parses an enum `T`. This errors at compile time, if the given enum
1308 ## type contains multiple fields with the same string value.
1309 ##
1310 ## Raises `ValueError` for an invalid value in `s`. The comparison is
1311 ## done in a style insensitive way (first letter is still case-sensitive).
1312 runnableExamples:
1313 type
1314 MyEnum = enum
1315 first = "1st",
1316 second,
1317 third = "3rd"
1318
1319 doAssert parseEnum[MyEnum]("1_st") == first
1320 doAssert parseEnum[MyEnum]("second") == second
1321 doAssertRaises(ValueError):
1322 echo parseEnum[MyEnum]("third")
1323
1324 ✗ genEnumCaseStmt(T, s, default = nil, ord(low(T)), ord(high(T)), nimIdentNormalize)
1325
1326 func parseEnum*[T: enum](s: string, default: T): T =
1327 ## Parses an enum `T`. This errors at compile time, if the given enum
1328 ## type contains multiple fields with the same string value.
1329 ##
1330 ## Uses `default` for an invalid value in `s`. The comparison is done in a
1331 ## style insensitive way (first letter is still case-sensitive).
1332 runnableExamples:
1333 type
1334 MyEnum = enum
1335 first = "1st",
1336 second,
1337 third = "3rd"
1338
1339 doAssert parseEnum[MyEnum]("1_st") == first
1340 doAssert parseEnum[MyEnum]("second") == second
1341 doAssert parseEnum[MyEnum]("last", third) == third
1342
1343 genEnumCaseStmt(T, s, default, ord(low(T)), ord(high(T)), nimIdentNormalize)
1344
1345 ✗ func repeat*(c: char, count: Natural): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuRepeatChar".} =
1346 ## Returns a string of length `count` consisting only of
1347 ## the character `c`.
1348 runnableExamples:
1349 let a = 'z'
1350 doAssert a.repeat(5) == "zzzzz"
1351 ✗ result = newString(count)
1352 ✗ for i in 0..count-1: result[i] = c
1353
1354 func repeat*(s: string, n: Natural): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuRepeatStr".} =
1355 ## Returns string `s` concatenated `n` times.
1356 runnableExamples:
1357 doAssert "+ foo +".repeat(3) == "+ foo ++ foo ++ foo +"
1358
1359 result = newStringOfCap(n * s.len)
1360 for i in 1..n: result.add(s)
1361
1362 func spaces*(n: Natural): string {.inline.} =
1363 ## Returns a string with `n` space characters. You can use this func
1364 ## to left align strings.
1365 ##
1366 ## See also:
1367 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1368 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1369 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1370 ## * `center func<#center,string,int,char>`_
1371 runnableExamples:
1372 let
1373 width = 15
1374 text1 = "Hello user!"
1375 text2 = "This is a very long string"
1376 doAssert text1 & spaces(max(0, width - text1.len)) & "|" ==
1377 "Hello user! |"
1378 doAssert text2 & spaces(max(0, width - text2.len)) & "|" ==
1379 "This is a very long string|"
1380 repeat(' ', n)
1381
1382 ✗ func align*(s: string, count: Natural, padding = ' '): string {.rtl,
1383 extern: "nsuAlignString".} =
1384 ## Aligns a string `s` with `padding`, so that it is of length `count`.
1385 ##
1386 ## `padding` characters (by default spaces) are added before `s` resulting in
1387 ## right alignment. If `s.len >= count`, no spaces are added and `s` is
1388 ## returned unchanged. If you need to left align a string use the `alignLeft
1389 ## func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_.
1390 ##
1391 ## See also:
1392 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1393 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1394 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1395 ## * `center func<#center,string,int,char>`_
1396 runnableExamples:
1397 assert align("abc", 4) == " abc"
1398 assert align("a", 0) == "a"
1399 assert align("1232", 6) == " 1232"
1400 assert align("1232", 6, '#') == "##1232"
1401 ✗ if s.len < count:
1402 ✗ result = newString(count)
1403 ✗ let spaces = count - s.len
1404 ✗ for i in 0..spaces-1: result[i] = padding
1405 ✗ for i in spaces..count-1: result[i] = s[i-spaces]
1406 else:
1407 result = s
1408
1409 29 func alignLeft*(s: string, count: Natural, padding = ' '): string =
1410 ## Left-Aligns a string `s` with `padding`, so that it is of length `count`.
1411 ##
1412 ## `padding` characters (by default spaces) are added after `s` resulting in
1413 ## left alignment. If `s.len >= count`, no spaces are added and `s` is
1414 ## returned unchanged. If you need to right align a string use the `align
1415 ## func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_.
1416 ##
1417 ## See also:
1418 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1419 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1420 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1421 ## * `center func<#center,string,int,char>`_
1422 runnableExamples:
1423 assert alignLeft("abc", 4) == "abc "
1424 assert alignLeft("a", 0) == "a"
1425 assert alignLeft("1232", 6) == "1232 "
1426 assert alignLeft("1232", 6, '#') == "1232##"
1427
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 29 times.
 29 if s.len < count:
1428 ✗ result = newString(count)
1429 ✗ if s.len > 0:
1430 ✗ result[0 .. (s.len - 1)] = s
1431 ✗ for i in s.len ..< count:
1432 ✗ result[i] = padding
1433 else:
1434 result = s
1435
1436 func center*(s: string, width: int, fillChar: char = ' '): string {.rtl,
1437 extern: "nsuCenterString".} =
1438 ## Return the contents of `s` centered in a string `width` long using
1439 ## `fillChar` (default: space) as padding.
1440 ##
1441 ## The original string is returned if `width` is less than or equal
1442 ## to `s.len`.
1443 ##
1444 ## See also:
1445 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1446 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1447 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1448 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1449 runnableExamples:
1450 let a = "foo"
1451 doAssert a.center(2) == "foo"
1452 doAssert a.center(5) == " foo "
1453 doAssert a.center(6) == " foo "
1454 if width <= s.len: return s
1455 result = newString(width)
1456 # Left padding will be one fillChar
1457 # smaller if there are an odd number
1458 # of characters
1459 let
1460 charsLeft = (width - s.len)
1461 leftPadding = charsLeft div 2
1462 for i in 0 ..< width:
1463 if i >= leftPadding and i < leftPadding + s.len:
1464 # we are where the string should be located
1465 result[i] = s[i-leftPadding]
1466 else:
1467 # we are either before or after where
1468 # the string s should go
1469 result[i] = fillChar
1470
1471 func indent*(s: string, count: Natural, padding: string = " "): string {.rtl,
1472 extern: "nsuIndent".} =
1473 ## Indents each line in `s` by `count` amount of `padding`.
1474 ##
1475 ## **Note:** This does not preserve the new line characters used in `s`.
1476 ##
1477 ## See also:
1478 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1479 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1480 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1481 ## * `unindent func<#unindent,string,Natural,string>`_
1482 ## * `dedent func<#dedent,string,Natural>`_
1483 runnableExamples:
1484 doAssert indent("First line\c\l and second line.", 2) ==
1485 " First line\l and second line."
1486 result = ""
1487 var i = 0
1488 for line in s.splitLines():
1489 if i != 0:
1490 result.add("\n")
1491 for j in 1..count:
1492 result.add(padding)
1493 result.add(line)
1494 i.inc
1495
1496 func unindent*(s: string, count: Natural = int.high,
1497 padding: string = " "): string {.rtl, extern: "nsuUnindent".} =
1498 ## Unindents each line in `s` by `count` amount of `padding`.
1499 ##
1500 ## **Note:** This does not preserve the new line characters used in `s`.
1501 ##
1502 ## See also:
1503 ## * `dedent func<#dedent,string,Natural>`_
1504 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1505 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1506 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1507 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1508 runnableExamples:
1509 let x = """
1510 Hello
1511 There
1512 """.unindent()
1513
1514 doAssert x == "Hello\nThere\n"
1515 result = ""
1516 var i = 0
1517 for line in s.splitLines():
1518 if i != 0:
1519 result.add("\n")
1520 var indentCount = 0
1521 for j in 0..<count.int:
1522 indentCount.inc
1523 if j + padding.len-1 >= line.len or line[j .. j + padding.len-1] != padding:
1524 indentCount = j
1525 break
1526 result.add(line[indentCount*padding.len .. ^1])
1527 i.inc
1528
1529 func indentation*(s: string): Natural {.since: (1, 3).} =
1530 ## Returns the amount of indentation all lines of `s` have in common,
1531 ## ignoring lines that consist only of whitespace.
1532 result = int.high
1533 for line in s.splitLines:
1534 for i, c in line:
1535 if i >= result: break
1536 elif c != ' ':
1537 result = i
1538 break
1539 if result == int.high:
1540 result = 0
1541
1542 func dedent*(s: string, count: Natural = indentation(s)): string {.rtl,
1543 extern: "nsuDedent", since: (1, 3).} =
1544 ## Unindents each line in `s` by `count` amount of `padding`.
1545 ## The only difference between this and the
1546 ## `unindent func<#unindent,string,Natural,string>`_ is that this by default
1547 ## only cuts off the amount of indentation that all lines of `s` share as
1548 ## opposed to all indentation. It only supports spaces as padding.
1549 ##
1550 ## **Note:** This does not preserve the new line characters used in `s`.
1551 ##
1552 ## See also:
1553 ## * `unindent func<#unindent,string,Natural,string>`_
1554 ## * `align func<#align,string,Natural,char>`_
1555 ## * `alignLeft func<#alignLeft,string,Natural,char>`_
1556 ## * `spaces func<#spaces,Natural>`_
1557 ## * `indent func<#indent,string,Natural,string>`_
1558 runnableExamples:
1559 let x = """
1560 Hello
1561 There
1562 """.dedent()
1563
1564 doAssert x == "Hello\n There\n"
1565 unindent(s, count, " ")
1566
1567 func delete*(s: var string, slice: Slice[int]) =
1568 ## Deletes the items `s[slice]`, raising `IndexDefect` if the slice contains
1569 ## elements out of range.
1570 ##
1571 ## This operation moves all elements after `s[slice]` in linear time, and
1572 ## is the string analog to `sequtils.delete`.
1573 runnableExamples:
1574 var a = "abcde"
1575 doAssertRaises(IndexDefect): a.delete(4..5)
1576 assert a == "abcde"
1577 a.delete(4..4)
1578 assert a == "abcd"
1579 a.delete(1..2)
1580 assert a == "ad"
1581 a.delete(1..<1) # empty slice
1582 assert a == "ad"
1583 when compileOption("boundChecks"):
1584 if not (slice.a < s.len and slice.a >= 0 and slice.b < s.len):
1585 raise newException(IndexDefect, $(slice: slice, len: s.len))
1586 if slice.b >= slice.a:
1587 var i = slice.a
1588 var j = slice.b + 1
1589 var newLen = s.len - j + i
1590 # if j < s.len: moveMem(addr s[i], addr s[j], s.len - j) # pending benchmark
1591 while i < newLen:
1592 s[i] = s[j]
1593 inc(i)
1594 inc(j)
1595 setLen(s, newLen)
1596
1597 func delete*(s: var string, first, last: int) {.rtl, extern: "nsuDelete",
1598 deprecated: "use `delete(s, first..last)`".} =
1599 ## Deletes in `s` the characters at positions `first .. last` (both ends included).
1600 runnableExamples("--warning:deprecated:off"):
1601 var a = "abracadabra"
1602
1603 a.delete(4, 5)
1604 doAssert a == "abradabra"
1605
1606 a.delete(1, 6)
1607 doAssert a == "ara"
1608
1609 a.delete(2, 999)
1610 doAssert a == "ar"
1611
1612 var i = first
1613 var j = min(len(s), last+1)
1614 var newLen = len(s)-j+i
1615 while i < newLen:
1616 s[i] = s[j]
1617 inc(i)
1618 inc(j)
1619 setLen(s, newLen)
1620
1621 func startsWith*(s: string, prefix: char): bool {.inline.} =
1622 ## Returns true if `s` starts with character `prefix`.
1623 ##
1624 ## See also:
1625 ## * `endsWith func<#endsWith,string,char>`_
1626 ## * `continuesWith func<#continuesWith,string,string,Natural>`_
1627 ## * `removePrefix func<#removePrefix,string,char>`_
1628 runnableExamples:
1629 let a = "abracadabra"
1630 doAssert a.startsWith('a') == true
1631 doAssert a.startsWith('b') == false
1632 result = s.len > 0 and s[0] == prefix
1633
1634 ✗ func startsWith*(s, prefix: string): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuStartsWith".} =
1635 ## Returns true if `s` starts with string `prefix`.
1636 ##
1637 ## If `prefix == ""` true is returned.
1638 ##
1639 ## See also:
1640 ## * `endsWith func<#endsWith,string,string>`_
1641 ## * `continuesWith func<#continuesWith,string,string,Natural>`_
1642 ## * `removePrefix func<#removePrefix,string,string>`_
1643 runnableExamples:
1644 let a = "abracadabra"
1645 doAssert a.startsWith("abra") == true
1646 doAssert a.startsWith("bra") == false
1647 ✗ result = false
1648 ✗ startsWithImpl(s, prefix)
1649
1650 func endsWith*(s: string, suffix: char): bool {.inline.} =
1651 ## Returns true if `s` ends with `suffix`.
1652 ##
1653 ## See also:
1654 ## * `startsWith func<#startsWith,string,char>`_
1655 ## * `continuesWith func<#continuesWith,string,string,Natural>`_
1656 ## * `removeSuffix func<#removeSuffix,string,char>`_
1657 runnableExamples:
1658 let a = "abracadabra"
1659 doAssert a.endsWith('a') == true
1660 doAssert a.endsWith('b') == false
1661 result = s.len > 0 and s[s.high] == suffix
1662
1663 ✗ func endsWith*(s, suffix: string): bool {.rtl, extern: "nsuEndsWith".} =
1664 ## Returns true if `s` ends with `suffix`.
1665 ##
1666 ## If `suffix == ""` true is returned.
1667 ##
1668 ## See also:
1669 ## * `startsWith func<#startsWith,string,string>`_
1670 ## * `continuesWith func<#continuesWith,string,string,Natural>`_
1671 ## * `removeSuffix func<#removeSuffix,string,string>`_
1672 runnableExamples:
1673 let a = "abracadabra"
1674 doAssert a.endsWith("abra") == true
1675 doAssert a.endsWith("dab") == false
1676 ✗ result = false
1677 ✗ endsWithImpl(s, suffix)
1678
1679 func continuesWith*(s, substr: string, start: Natural): bool {.rtl,
1680 extern: "nsuContinuesWith".} =
1681 ## Returns true if `s` continues with `substr` at position `start`.
1682 ##
1683 ## If `substr == ""` true is returned.
1684 ##
1685 ## See also:
1686 ## * `startsWith func<#startsWith,string,string>`_
1687 ## * `endsWith func<#endsWith,string,string>`_
1688 runnableExamples:
1689 let a = "abracadabra"
1690 doAssert a.continuesWith("ca", 4) == true
1691 doAssert a.continuesWith("ca", 5) == false
1692 doAssert a.continuesWith("dab", 6) == true
1693 result = false
1694 var i = 0
1695 while true:
1696 if i >= substr.len: return true
1697 if i+start >= s.len or s[i+start] != substr[i]: return false
1698 inc(i)
1699
1700
1701 func removePrefix*(s: var string, chars: set[char] = Newlines) {.rtl,
1702 extern: "nsuRemovePrefixCharSet".} =
1703 ## Removes all characters from `chars` from the start of the string `s`
1704 ## (in-place).
1705 ##
1706 ## See also:
1707 ## * `removeSuffix func<#removeSuffix,string,set[char]>`_
1708 runnableExamples:
1709 var userInput = "\r\n*~Hello World!"
1710 userInput.removePrefix
1711 doAssert userInput == "*~Hello World!"
1712 userInput.removePrefix({'~', '*'})
1713 doAssert userInput == "Hello World!"
1714
1715 var otherInput = "?!?Hello!?!"
1716 otherInput.removePrefix({'!', '?'})
1717 doAssert otherInput == "Hello!?!"
1718
1719 var start = 0
1720 while start < s.len and s[start] in chars: start += 1
1721 if start > 0: s.delete(0..start - 1)
1722
1723 func removePrefix*(s: var string, c: char) {.rtl,
1724 extern: "nsuRemovePrefixChar".} =
1725 ## Removes all occurrences of a single character (in-place) from the start
1726 ## of a string.
1727 ##
1728 ## See also:
1729 ## * `removeSuffix func<#removeSuffix,string,char>`_
1730 ## * `startsWith func<#startsWith,string,char>`_
1731 runnableExamples:
1732 var ident = "pControl"
1733 ident.removePrefix('p')
1734 doAssert ident == "Control"
1735 removePrefix(s, chars = {c})
1736
1737 func removePrefix*(s: var string, prefix: string) {.rtl,
1738 extern: "nsuRemovePrefixString".} =
1739 ## Remove the first matching prefix (in-place) from a string.
1740 ##
1741 ## See also:
1742 ## * `removeSuffix func<#removeSuffix,string,string>`_
1743 ## * `startsWith func<#startsWith,string,string>`_
1744 runnableExamples:
1745 var answers = "yesyes"
1746 answers.removePrefix("yes")
1747 doAssert answers == "yes"
1748 if s.startsWith(prefix) and prefix.len > 0:
1749 s.delete(0..prefix.len - 1)
1750
1751 func removeSuffix*(s: var string, chars: set[char] = Newlines) {.rtl,
1752 extern: "nsuRemoveSuffixCharSet".} =
1753 ## Removes all characters from `chars` from the end of the string `s`
1754 ## (in-place).
1755 ##
1756 ## See also:
1757 ## * `removePrefix func<#removePrefix,string,set[char]>`_
1758 runnableExamples:
1759 var userInput = "Hello World!*~\r\n"
1760 userInput.removeSuffix
1761 doAssert userInput == "Hello World!*~"
1762 userInput.removeSuffix({'~', '*'})
1763 doAssert userInput == "Hello World!"
1764
1765 var otherInput = "Hello!?!"
1766 otherInput.removeSuffix({'!', '?'})
1767 doAssert otherInput == "Hello"
1768
1769 if s.len == 0: return
1770 var last = s.high
1771 while last > -1 and s[last] in chars: last -= 1
1772 s.setLen(last + 1)
1773
1774 func removeSuffix*(s: var string, c: char) {.rtl,
1775 extern: "nsuRemoveSuffixChar".} =
1776 ## Removes all occurrences of a single character (in-place) from the end
1777 ## of a string.
1778 ##
1779 ## See also:
1780 ## * `removePrefix func<#removePrefix,string,char>`_
1781 ## * `endsWith func<#endsWith,string,char>`_
1782 runnableExamples:
1783 var table = "users"
1784 table.removeSuffix('s')
1785 doAssert table == "user"
1786
1787 var dots = "Trailing dots......."
1788 dots.removeSuffix('.')
1789 doAssert dots == "Trailing dots"
1790
1791 removeSuffix(s, chars = {c})
1792
1793 func removeSuffix*(s: var string, suffix: string) {.rtl,
1794 extern: "nsuRemoveSuffixString".} =
1795 ## Remove the first matching suffix (in-place) from a string.
1796 ##
1797 ## See also:
1798 ## * `removePrefix func<#removePrefix,string,string>`_
1799 ## * `endsWith func<#endsWith,string,string>`_
1800 runnableExamples:
1801 var answers = "yeses"
1802 answers.removeSuffix("es")
1803 doAssert answers == "yes"
1804 var newLen = s.len
1805 if s.endsWith(suffix):
1806 newLen -= len(suffix)
1807 s.setLen(newLen)
1808
1809
1810 func addSep*(dest: var string, sep = ", ", startLen: Natural = 0) {.inline.} =
1811 ## Adds a separator to `dest` only if its length is bigger than `startLen`.
1812 ##
1813 ## A shorthand for:
1814 ##
1815 ## ```nim
1816 ## if dest.len > startLen: add(dest, sep)
1817 ## ```
1818 ##
1819 ## This is often useful for generating some code where the items need to
1820 ## be *separated* by `sep`. `sep` is only added if `dest` is longer than
1821 ## `startLen`. The following example creates a string describing
1822 ## an array of integers.
1823 runnableExamples:
1824 var arr = "["
1825 for x in items([2, 3, 5, 7, 11]):
1826 addSep(arr, startLen = len("["))
1827 add(arr, $x)
1828 add(arr, "]")
1829 doAssert arr == "[2, 3, 5, 7, 11]"
1830
1831 if dest.len > startLen: add(dest, sep)
1832
1833 ✗ func allCharsInSet*(s: string, theSet: set[char]): bool =
1834 ## Returns true if every character of `s` is in the set `theSet`.
1835 runnableExamples:
1836 doAssert allCharsInSet("aeea", {'a', 'e'}) == true
1837 doAssert allCharsInSet("", {'a', 'e'}) == true
1838
1839 ✗ for c in items(s):
1840 ✗ if c notin theSet: return false
1841 ✗ return true
1842
1843 ✗ func abbrev*(s: string, possibilities: openArray[string]): int =
1844 ## Returns the index of the first item in `possibilities` which starts
1845 ## with `s`, if not ambiguous.
1846 ##
1847 ## Returns -1 if no item has been found and -2 if multiple items match.
1848 runnableExamples:
1849 doAssert abbrev("fac", ["college", "faculty", "industry"]) == 1
1850 doAssert abbrev("foo", ["college", "faculty", "industry"]) == -1 # Not found
1851 doAssert abbrev("fac", ["college", "faculty", "faculties"]) == -2 # Ambiguous
1852 doAssert abbrev("college", ["college", "colleges", "industry"]) == 0
1853
1854 result = -1 # none found
1855 for i in 0..possibilities.len-1:
1856 if possibilities[i].startsWith(s):
1857 if possibilities[i] == s:
1858 # special case: exact match shouldn't be ambiguous
1859 return i
1860 if result >= 0: return -2 # ambiguous
1861 result = i
1862
1863 # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
1864
1865 ✗ func join*(a: openArray[string], sep: string = ""): string {.rtl,
1866 extern: "nsuJoinSep".} =
1867 ## Concatenates all strings in the container `a`, separating them with `sep`.
1868 runnableExamples:
1869 doAssert join(["A", "B", "Conclusion"], " -> ") == "A -> B -> Conclusion"
1870
1871 ✗ if len(a) > 0:
1872 ✗ var L = sep.len * (a.len-1)
1873 ✗ for i in 0..high(a): inc(L, a[i].len)
1874 ✗ result = newStringOfCap(L)
1875 ✗ add(result, a[0])
1876 ✗ for i in 1..high(a):
1877 ✗ add(result, sep)
1878 ✗ add(result, a[i])
1879 else:
1880 ✗ result = ""
1881
1882 ✗ proc join*[T: not string](a: openArray[T], sep: string = ""): string =
1883 ✗ ## Converts all elements in the container `a` to strings using `$`,
1884 ## and concatenates them with `sep`.
1885 runnableExamples:
1886 doAssert join([1, 2, 3], " -> ") == "1 -> 2 -> 3"
1887
1888 result = ""
1889 for i, x in a:
1890 if i > 0:
1891 add(result, sep)
1892 add(result, $x)
1893
1894 type
1895 SkipTable* = array[char, int] ## Character table for efficient substring search.
1896
1897 ✗ func initSkipTable*(a: var SkipTable, sub: string) {.rtl,
1898 extern: "nsuInitSkipTable".} =
1899 ## Initializes table `a` for efficient search of substring `sub`.
1900 ##
1901 ## See also:
1902 ## * `initSkipTable func<#initSkipTable,string>`_
1903 ## * `find func<#find,SkipTable,string,string,Natural,int>`_
1904 # TODO: this should be the `default()` initializer for the type.
1905 ✗ let m = len(sub)
1906 ✗ fill(a, m)
1907 ✗
1908 ✗ for i in 0 ..< m - 1:
1909 ✗ a[sub[i]] = m - 1 - i
1910
1911 ✗ func initSkipTable*(sub: string): SkipTable {.noinit, rtl,
1912 extern: "nsuInitNewSkipTable".} =
1913 ## Returns a new table initialized for `sub`.
1914 ##
1915 ## See also:
1916 ## * `initSkipTable func<#initSkipTable,SkipTable,string>`_
1917 ## * `find func<#find,SkipTable,string,string,Natural,int>`_
1918 ✗ initSkipTable(result, sub)
1919 ✗
1920 ✗ func find*(a: SkipTable, s, sub: string, start: Natural = 0, last = -1): int {.
1921 rtl, extern: "nsuFindStrA".} =
1922 ## Searches for `sub` in `s` inside range `start..last` using preprocessed
1923 ## table `a`. If `last` is unspecified, it defaults to `s.high` (the last
1924 ## element).
1925 ##
1926 ## Searching is case-sensitive. If `sub` is not in `s`, -1 is returned.
1927 ##
1928 ## See also:
1929 ## * `initSkipTable func<#initSkipTable,string>`_
1930 ## * `initSkipTable func<#initSkipTable,SkipTable,string>`_
1931 let
1932 last = if last < 0: s.high else: last
1933 subLast = sub.len - 1
1934
1935 ✗ if subLast == -1:
1936 # this was an empty needle string,
1937 # we count this as match in the first possible position:
1938 ✗ return start
1939
1940 # This is an implementation of the Boyer-Moore Horspool algorithms
1941 # https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boyer%E2%80%93Moore%E2%80%93Horspool_algorithm
1942 ✗ result = -1
1943 ✗ var skip = start
1944 ✗
1945 ✗ while last - skip >= subLast:
1946 ✗ var i = subLast
1947 ✗ while s[skip + i] == sub[i]:
1948 ✗ if i == 0:
1949 ✗ return skip
1950 ✗ dec i
1951 ✗ inc skip, a[s[skip + subLast]]
1952
1953 ✗ when not (defined(js) or defined(nimdoc) or defined(nimscript)):
1954 ✗ from system/ansi_c import c_memchr
1955
1956 const hasCStringBuiltin = true
1957 else:
1958 const hasCStringBuiltin = false
1959
1960 ✗ func find*(s: string, sub: char, start: Natural = 0, last = -1): int {.rtl,
1961 extern: "nsuFindChar".} =
1962 ## Searches for `sub` in `s` inside range `start..last` (both ends included).
1963 ## If `last` is unspecified or negative, it defaults to `s.high` (the last element).
1964 ##
1965 ## Searching is case-sensitive. If `sub` is not in `s`, -1 is returned.
1966 ## Otherwise the index returned is relative to `s[0]`, not `start`.
1967 ## Subtract `start` from the result for a `start`-origin index.
1968 ##
1969 ## See also:
1970 ## * `rfind func<#rfind,string,char,Natural,int>`_
1971 ## * `replace func<#replace,string,char,char>`_
1972 ✗ result = -1
1973 ✗ let last = if last < 0: s.high else: last
1974 ✗
1975 template findImpl =
1976 ✗ for i in int(start)..last:
1977 if s[i] == sub:
1978 ✗ return i
1979
1980 when nimvm:
1981 findImpl()
1982 else:
1983 when hasCStringBuiltin:
1984 ✗ let length = last-start+1
1985 ✗ if length > 0:
1986 ✗ let found = c_memchr(s[start].unsafeAddr, cint(sub), cast[csize_t](length))
1987 ✗ if not found.isNil:
1988 ✗ return cast[int](found) -% cast[int](s.cstring)
1989 else:
1990 findImpl()
1991 ✗
1992 ✗ func find*(s: string, chars: set[char], start: Natural = 0, last = -1): int {.
1993 ✗ rtl, extern: "nsuFindCharSet".} =
1994 ✗ ## Searches for `chars` in `s` inside range `start..last` (both ends included).
1995 ## If `last` is unspecified or negative, it defaults to `s.high` (the last element).
1996 ##
1997 ## If `s` contains none of the characters in `chars`, -1 is returned.
1998 ## Otherwise the index returned is relative to `s[0]`, not `start`.
1999 ## Subtract `start` from the result for a `start`-origin index.
2000 ##
2001 ## See also:
2002 ## * `rfind func<#rfind,string,set[char],Natural,int>`_
2003 ## * `multiReplace func<#multiReplace,string,varargs[]>`_
2004 ✗ result = -1
2005 ✗ let last = if last < 0: s.high else: last
2006 ✗ for i in int(start)..last:
2007 ✗ if s[i] in chars:
2008 ✗ return i
2009
2010 when defined(linux):
2011 proc memmem(haystack: pointer, haystacklen: csize_t,
2012 needle: pointer, needlelen: csize_t): pointer {.importc, header: """#ifndef _GNU_SOURCE
2013 #define _GNU_SOURCE
2014 #endif
2015 #include <string.h>""".}
2016 elif defined(bsd) or (defined(macosx) and not defined(ios)):
2017 proc memmem(haystack: pointer, haystacklen: csize_t,
2018 needle: pointer, needlelen: csize_t): pointer {.importc, header: "#include <string.h>".}
2019
2020 23 func find*(s, sub: string, start: Natural = 0, last = -1): int {.rtl,
2021 extern: "nsuFindStr".} =
2022 ## Searches for `sub` in `s` inside range `start..last` (both ends included).
2023 ## If `last` is unspecified or negative, it defaults to `s.high` (the last element).
2024 ##
2025 ## Searching is case-sensitive. If `sub` is not in `s`, -1 is returned.
2026 ## Otherwise the index returned is relative to `s[0]`, not `start`.
2027 ## Subtract `start` from the result for a `start`-origin index.
2028 ##
2029 ## See also:
2030 ## * `rfind func<#rfind,string,string,Natural,int>`_
2031 ## * `replace func<#replace,string,string,string>`_
2032
